title: Henry IV, Parts I & II
author: William Shakespeare
quotesVerified: The Complete Works of William Shakespeare (modern-spelling), Project Gutenberg #100 - both Parts I and II.
overture: A wastrel prince drinks in an Eastcheap tavern while, in the north, the Percys who put his father on the throne rise to pull him off it. Two plays, one arc: the realm converging on the field at Shrewsbury, a fat knight shadowing the wars he mocks, and Prince Hal at last throwing off his riot - and the old companion with it - to be crowned Henry V.
mapNote: No map survives from Shakespeare's England of 1403, so the Victorian survey stays off for this one; the places are plotted on the modern land, whose coastline and rivers are essentially unchanged.
sources: Holinshed's Chronicles (Shakespeare's source for the reign, the Battle of Shrewsbury, and the tripartite indenture at Bangor).
The historic road network is the surviving Roman/medieval line: Watling Street, the Dover Road, Ermin Way and the Great North Road, as they stood c.1403.

chapters.title: Part I, Act I - the court and the tavern
Part I, Act II - Gad's Hill
Part I, Act III - the kingdom divided
Part I, Act IV - the muster
Part I, Act V - Shrewsbury field
Part II, Act I - the north in mourning
Part II, Act II - back to the Boar's Head
Part II, Act III - the sleepless king
Part II, Act IV - Gaultree, and a deathbed
Part II, Act V - the new-crowned king

characters.name: Prince Hal
characters.role: The truant Prince of Wales who shuttles between tavern and throne, kills Hotspur at Shrewsbury, and hardens into King Henry V - casting off Falstaff at the coronation.

characters.name: Falstaff
characters.role: Sir John Falstaff: fat knight, thief and false soldier - the tavern's gravity, dragged twice to wars he mocks, and finally rejected by the king he taught to play.

characters.name: Hotspur
characters.role: Harry Percy, honour-mad rebel and the play's anti-Hal - from the court quarrel to the division of the kingdom to death on Shrewsbury field.

exit.note: Hal kills Hotspur hand to hand on the field north of Shrewsbury, and finishes the dying man's broken line for him: 'for worms, brave Percy'. The body is on stage for the rest of the act, and Falstaff carries it off claiming the kill.

characters.name: King Henry IV
characters.role: The usurper-king, careworn and guilt-ridden; he wins Shrewsbury, sickens, and dies in the Jerusalem Chamber as his son lifts the crown.

exit.note: The King collapses at Westminster and is carried into the Jerusalem Chamber, where he is reconciled with the son who lifted the crown from his pillow, and dies there - the prophecy that he should die in Jerusalem kept, and cheated.

characters.name: Archbishop Scroop
characters.role: Richard Scroop, Archbishop of York, who inherits the broken Percy rebellion in Part II and is destroyed by Prince John's kept-and-broken word at Gaultree.

exit.note: The instant his army has scattered, Scroop is arrested at Gaultree for capital treason and sent straight to the block: 'some guard these traitors to the block of death'. Shakespeare does not stage the execution or say where the block stood, but Gaultree is where the play stops him for good; Westmoreland reports the rebel leaders taken two acts later.

characters.name: Prince John
characters.role: Prince John of Lancaster, Hal's cold, able younger brother - blooded at Shrewsbury, then the architect of the Gaultree betrayal.

characters.name: Northumberland
characters.role: The Earl of Northumberland, Hotspur's father - whose failure to bring his army to Shrewsbury dooms his son, and whose grief and flight to Scotland open the second play.

exit.note: Warkworth is the last place the play puts Northumberland on stage. Talked out of marching by his son's widow, he resolves for Scotland - 'there am I, till time and vantage crave my company' - and Shakespeare never follows him there. Word that he is 'by the sheriff of Yorkshire overthrown' reaches Westminster two acts later as a line of second-hand news, and the play never says he died.

locations.name: Eastcheap, City of London
locations.story: The Boar's Head, Falstaff's kingdom of sack and lies. Here Prince Hal idles among thieves and drawers, plays out a mock-interview with his father, and speaks the cold aside that one day he will cast all this off.
locations.quote: Banish plump Jack, and banish all the world.

image.caption: Falstaff holds forth in the Boar's Head, Eastcheap (1 Henry IV, II.iv); steel engraving, 1825.
image.credit: Robert Smirke R.A., engraved by Charles Heath, 1825 · The Metropolitan Museum of Art · public domain

locations.name: Westminster, London
locations.story: The court, and the crown that will not rest easy. A careworn Henry IV reproves his truant son, wins his war and grieves his health away, and dies at last in the Jerusalem Chamber as Hal lifts the crown from his pillow.
locations.quote: Uneasy lies the head that wears a crown.

image.caption: Westminster Abbey and Westminster Bridge from the Thames, with river craft; aquatint, 1810 - four centuries later than the play, like the map beneath it.
image.credit: After Rowlandson & Pugin, from Ackermann's Microcosm of London, 1810 · public domain

locations.name: Gad's Hill, Kent
locations.story: On the old Dover Road, Falstaff and his fellows rob the King's pilgrims at dawn - and are robbed in turn by Hal and Poins in disguise, for the sport of hearing Falstaff lie about it afterward.
locations.quote: The thieves have bound the true men.

image.caption: The carriers and conspirators before dawn at Gad's Hill (1 Henry IV, II.i); painted for the Boydell Shakespeare gallery.
image.credit: Robert Smirke R.A. (1753–1845), Boydell Shakespeare series · Royal Shakespeare Theatre collection · public domain

locations.name: Warkworth Castle, Northumberland
locations.story: The Percy stronghold in the north. Here Hotspur reads a wavering lord's letter and teases his wife Kate before riding to the rising; and here, in the second play, his father hears of his death and is talked into flight.
locations.quote: Out of this nettle, danger, we pluck this flower, safety.

image.caption: The ruined keep and towers of Warkworth Castle, the Percy stronghold; engraving, 1814.
image.credit: J. Greig after L. Clennell, from The Border Antiquities of England and Scotland, 1814 · public domain

locations.name: Bangor, Gwynedd
locations.note: The division-of-the-kingdom scene (Part I, III.1). The Folio gives no locality; the setting at the Archdeacon of Bangor's house comes from Holinshed and editorial tradition, so the town is real but named for the scene only by that tradition.
locations.story: In Wales the confederates spread a map and carve the kingdom into three - Hotspur quarrelling that the winding Trent has cheated his share of the land, and demanding the river's course be turned.
locations.quote: See how this river comes me cranking in, And cuts me from the best of all my land.
locations.imageBlank: The division-of-the-kingdom scene is placed at Bangor only by editorial tradition, and no honest period view of the spot was to hand - better a blank than a borrowed one.

locations.name: Battlefield, north of Shrewsbury
locations.note: The Battle of Shrewsbury (21 July 1403) was fought at Battlefield, some three miles north of the town, where Battlefield church still stands to commemorate the dead; the pin marks the field, not the town centre.
locations.story: The field north of the town where the two Harrys meet at last. Hal kills Hotspur in single combat; Falstaff counterfeits death, then rises to claim the kill as his own; and the Percy rebellion breaks.
locations.quote: No, Percy, thou art dust, And food for-

image.caption: The death of Hotspur at the Battle of Shrewsbury, 1403; colour illustration, 1864.
image.credit: James William Edmund Doyle, A Chronicle of England, 1864 · public domain

locations.name: Coventry, Warwickshire
locations.story: On the march to Shrewsbury, Falstaff halts near Coventry - ashamed to be seen leading his scarecrow company of pressed and ragged men, 'food for powder', up to the field.
locations.quote: I'll not march through Coventry with them, that's flat.

image.caption: St Michael's, Coventry - the great spired medieval church by the road Falstaff's ragged company took; watercolour, 1802.
image.credit: William Crotch, 1802 · Norfolk Museums · public domain

locations.name: York
locations.story: The Archbishop of York takes up the broken rebellion, preaching a commonwealth sick of the king it chose, and marshalling a churchman's war in the north.
locations.quote: The commonwealth is sick of their own choice; Their over-greedy love hath surfeited.

image.caption: York, the Minster rising over the city walls - Archbishop Scroop's seat; photochrom, c.1890s.
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection · Library of Congress · public domain

locations.name: Forest of Galtres, north of York
locations.note: The royal Forest of Galtres was an area stretching north from the walls of York, not a single point; the pin is a central proxy within the historic forest, which the play (II.iv.1) names as the place of the parley.
locations.story: In the forest north of York the rebels are met, their grievances granted and their army sent home - and then, disarmed, arrested for treason. The coldest victory in the plays, and won by a broken word.
locations.quote: God, and not we, hath safely fought today.
locations.imageBlank: The Forest of Galtres is long gone and no period view of it, or of the Gaultree parley, was found - a logged blank rather than a stand-in.

locations.name: Gloucestershire
locations.note: The play sets Justice Shallow's house only 'in Gloucestershire' and never fixes it; this is a representative point on the Cotswold escarpment near Dursley, marked as a best guess, not an identified site.
locations.story: At Justice Shallow's, Falstaff presses recruits and remembers a wild youth - 'we have heard the chimes at midnight' - then, hearing the old King is dead, posts for London and the favour he is sure awaits him.
locations.quote: We have heard the chimes at midnight, Master Shallow.

image.caption: A Gloucestershire prospect over the Severn vale - an indicative view for Justice Shallow's unlocated house; oil, early 19th century.
image.credit: William Turner of Oxford (1789–1862), 'Newnham-on-Severn from Dean Hill' · Art UK · public domain

movements.routeSource: The Dover Road (Watling Street) east out of London - Blackheath, Dartford, Gravesend - to Gad's Hill by Rochester.
movements.note: Down to Kent for the robbery - Falstaff to rob the pilgrims, Hal and Poins in disguise to rob Falstaff.
Back to the Boar's Head, where Falstaff's two assailants swell to eleven buckram men in the telling.

movements.routeSource: The Thames between the City and Westminster - the ordinary way to move between the two in 1403 was by barge; the play does not specify.
movements.note: Summoned upriver to his father; the great reconciliation, 'I will redeem all this.'
Back down the river to rouse Falstaff and settle his charge of foot before the war.

movements.routeSource: Watling Street, the London-to-Wroxeter Roman road via St Albans, Towcester and Wall - the principal north-west route in 1403.
movements.note: Rides north with the royal army to the muster at Shrewsbury.

movements.routeSource: Watling Street, returning south to London after the battle.
movements.note: Back to London after Shrewsbury; we rediscover the prince at the tavern.
Called upriver to his dying father; he takes the crown from the pillow.

movements.routeSource: The London-to-Coventry road - Watling Street to Towcester, then the Daventry branch.
movements.note: Marching his ragged pressed company north; ashamed to be seen with them near Coventry.

movements.routeSource: On to Shrewsbury by the Watling Street line through Wall and Wellington.
movements.note: On toward the field with his doomed conscripts, 'food for powder'.

movements.routeSource: Watling Street back to London after the battle.
movements.note: Back to London, sparring with the Lord Chief Justice in the street.

movements.routeSource: The Oxford road west over the Chilterns, then Ermin Way toward the Cotswolds.
movements.note: Rides to Justice Shallow's to press recruits - Mouldy, Shadow, Wart, Feeble, Bullcalf.

movements.routeSource: No route named; a period-plausible ride up the Midlands to Yorkshire, assembled from the main towns.
movements.note: Arrives after the trap is sprung, in time to bag the fleeing Sir John Coleville.

movements.routeSource: Returning south to Gloucestershire by the same Midlands line.
movements.note: Detours back to Shallow's to feast and fleece him.

movements.routeSource: Ermin Way to Cirencester, then the Oxford-to-London road over the Chilterns.
movements.note: 'The laws of England are at my commandment' - posts to London for the coronation.

movements.routeSource: The Great North Road - Ware, Stamford, Doncaster, York, Durham, Newcastle - the main London-to-Scotland highway.
movements.note: Father and son ride home to the north to raise the Percy lands against the crown.

movements.routeSource: No route named; a period-plausible ride across the north to the confederates' council in Wales, via the western road through Carlisle and Chester.
movements.note: West across the country to Glendower's, for the council of the rebellion.

movements.routeSource: No route named; back east along the north-Wales coast to Chester and south to the muster at Shrewsbury.
movements.note: Marches to the rebel muster, his father sick and Glendower delayed.

movements.routeSource: Watling Street, the London-to-Wroxeter road, leading the royal host north.
movements.note: Leads the royal army north to meet the Percys.

movements.routeSource: Watling Street, returning to London after the victory.
movements.note: King and prince ride south with the victorious army - the King to sicken, John to be loosed on the next rising.

movements.routeSource: A short ride north from York into the royal Forest of Galtres.
movements.note: Leads the rebel army out into the forest to parley.

movements.routeSource: The Great North Road up to York and the Forest of Galtres.
movements.note: Marches north to face the Archbishop's rising.

movements.routeSource: The Great North Road back to court after the arrests.
movements.note: Rides back south in time for his father's death and the new reign.

story.title: The truant prince
story.narration: London, and a prince at play. Henry, Prince of Wales - Hal - idles his days in an Eastcheap tavern among thieves and drunkards, chief of them the vast, cowardly, irresistible Sir John Falstaff. But Hal is only waiting; one day, he means to throw all this off.

story.title: The uneasy crown
story.narration: A little way upriver, his father cannot rest. Henry IV took the throne by deposing and killing Richard II, and the guilt has never left him - and now word comes of a young hero on the borders he wishes were his son instead.

story.title: Hotspur at court
story.narration: That hero is Harry Percy - Hotspur - fresh from crushing the Scots at Holmedon. But when the King demands his prisoners, Hotspur refuses; and the Percys, who helped Henry to the crown, begin to plot to pull him from it.

story.title: The Gad's Hill jest
story.narration: Back among the low company, a jest is afoot. Falstaff and his fellows ride down the Dover Road to rob the King's pilgrims at Gad's Hill - while Hal and his madcap crony Poins, in disguise, plot to rob the robbers.

story.title: Eleven men in buckram
story.narration: The robbery done and undone, they gallop back to the Boar's Head - Falstaff roaring of the two men in buckram who set on him and who, by the time he reaches the tavern, have swollen to eleven.

story.title: Banish plump Jack
story.narration: The tavern's finest hour: caught in the lie, Falstaff only lies more gloriously. They play at king and prince by the fire, until he pleads his own case - 'banish plump Jack, and banish all the world' - and Hal answers, quietly, 'I do, I will.'

Meanwhile, the trouble that will one day undo all this jesting is already stirring. Wind back: slighted at court, Hotspur and his father Northumberland have turned for home, to raise the whole Percy north against the king they made.

story.title: North to Warkworth
story.narration: The road runs the length of England, up the Great North Road to the Percy stronghold at Warkworth, on the Northumberland coast.

story.title: All fire
story.narration: At Warkworth, Hotspur is all fire - scorning a wavering ally's letter, teasing his wife Kate, breathing danger like air: 'out of this nettle, danger, we pluck this flower, safety.' His father, though, is quietly sickening.

story.title: To Glendower's
story.narration: He rides west, clear across the country to Wales, to the last council of the rebels at the house of the wizard-lord Glendower.

story.title: The kingdom divided
story.narration: There they spread a map and carve England into three. Hotspur, maddened that the winding River Trent has cheated his third of the land, demands the river's course be changed: 'see how this river comes me cranking in, and cuts me from the best of all my land.'
Meanwhile, back in London, the King has at last sent for his son.

story.title: Summoned upriver
story.narration: Hal is carried by barge from Eastcheap up to the court at Westminster - out of the tavern's world and into the throne's.

story.title: Father and son
story.narration: Alone together at last. The King pours out his shame and his fear that his heir is lost; and Hal, stung to the quick, swears to redeem every wasted hour by meeting Hotspur in the field and taking his honours from him.

story.title: One last errand
story.narration: But first, back down the river to the tavern one last time - to haul Falstaff from his cups and hand him a company of foot for the coming war.

story.title: Captain Falstaff
story.narration: Falstaff, a captain now, pockets the King's press-money and means to make what profit he can of it. The tavern breaks up; the sack is set aside; the road leads north, to war.

story.title: The crown marches
story.narration: The crown marches. Henry IV leads the royal army up Watling Street, the old Roman road, toward the rebels massing in the west.
And the King is not alone on the roads. Wind back to the muster: out of the tavern at last, the prince rides north to join his father and make his word good.

story.title: Out of Eastcheap at last
story.narration: Hal takes the same north-west road to Shrewsbury, riding to meet the Hotspur whose honours he has sworn to win from him.
And from the west, the rebels come the other way.

story.title: The rebels close in
story.narration: Hotspur presses for battle though the news darkens: his father lies sick at Warkworth and will not march, and Glendower's Welshmen are delayed. Still he drives on to Shrewsbury.
And last, straggling up the Midlands road behind them all - the fat knight and his scarecrows.

story.title: Not through Coventry
story.narration: Falstaff marches a company of pressed and ragged men he is ashamed to be seen with: 'I'll not march through Coventry with them, that's flat.'

story.title: Food for powder
story.narration: He marches them to the field regardless - 'food for powder', he shrugs; they will fill a pit as well as better men.

story.title: The two Harrys
story.narration: On the field north of Shrewsbury the two Harrys meet at last. They fight, and Hal kills Hotspur hand to hand. The rebel's dying words break off - 'no, Percy, thou art dust, and food for-' - and Hal finishes them: 'for worms, brave Percy.'

story.title: Counterfeit
story.narration: Falstaff survives the only way he knows - by playing dead. Then he rises, stabs the corpse to be sure, hoists it on his back, and claims the killing as his own. The King's cold second son, Prince John, is blooded here in his first battle.

story.title: The north in mourning
story.narration: The second play opens on the reckoning. At Warkworth the news of Hotspur's death reaches his father; and the old Earl, who failed to bring his army to the field, fails his son once more - and flees to Scotland rather than fight on.

story.title: The Archbishop takes up arms
story.narration: But the rising does not die with the Percys. At York the Archbishop, Scroop, takes up the broken cause - a churchman turned rebel, preaching a commonwealth sick of the very king it chose.

story.title: The victors ride home
story.narration: Far to the south, the King and Prince John ride home with the victorious army - the King already carrying the sickness that will kill him, John already marked for the next rebellion.
And with them, though he keeps his own counsel and his own pace, the fat knight turns for home.

story.title: A counterfeit hero home
story.narration: Falstaff rides back to London a made man, a battle-hero on a lie, to take up his old life - and to trade insults in the street with the Lord Chief Justice, the one man in England unafraid of him.

story.title: Back to the old haunts
story.narration: The prince, too, drifts back to the tavern - but the old life has soured on him, and he comes half in disguise, watching more than joining.

story.title: The tavern grows old
story.narration: The Boar's Head is greyer now: Falstaff aging among the drawers, sparring with Doll Tearsheet, the tavern whore he dotes on, while the disguised prince looks on and hears his old companion mock him behind his back.

story.title: The sleepless king
story.narration: At Westminster the King lies awake through the small hours, sick and careworn, envying the poorest drenched sailor his rest: 'uneasy lies the head that wears a crown.'

story.title: Recruiting at Shallow's
story.narration: Sent to raise more men, Falstaff rides west into Gloucestershire, to the house of an old acquaintance - the country justice Shallow - to press a comic muster of yokels and pocket the bribes of those who can pay to stay home.

story.title: The chimes at midnight
story.narration: In Shallow's orchard, two old men remember a wildness that may never have been theirs - 'we have heard the chimes at midnight, Master Shallow' - while Falstaff privately reckons how much this foolish friend is worth to him.

story.title: The rebels take the field
story.narration: North again, to the last rebellion. The Archbishop leads his army out of York into the royal forest of Gaultree to parley - trusting the King's terms, and the King's cold son who brings them.

story.title: Into Gaultree Forest
story.narration: It is a short ride north from the city walls into the greenwood, where the two armies halt within sight of one another and the churchman lays out his grievances.

story.title: Prince John comes north
story.narration: Against him rides Prince John, up the Great North Road from Westminster - grave, pious, and utterly without scruple.

story.title: A broken word
story.narration: John grants every grievance, and the rebels, delighted, send their army home. The instant it has scattered he arrests their leaders for treason, to be executed out of hand: 'God, and not we, hath safely fought today.'
And Falstaff, ever late to a fight, was straggling north on his own road all the while.

story.title: Late to the war
story.narration: He reaches Gaultree only after the trap is sprung - just in time to take the surrender of one exhausted, fleeing rebel, and claim him as a mighty capture of his own.
Meanwhile, in London, a king is dying.

story.title: To the deathbed
story.narration: Hal is called upriver one last time, to his father's sickbed in the Jerusalem Chamber at Westminster.

story.title: The crown taken early
story.narration: Believing the King already dead, Hal lifts the crown from the pillow and carries it out. His father wakes and calls it theft - but the two are reconciled at the last, and the old King dies leaving his son the throne he stole.

story.title: Home for a death
story.narration: His northern work done, Prince John rides back down to court, in time for his father's death and his brother's crowning.

story.title: Back to feast
story.narration: Falstaff loops back to Shallow's Gloucestershire house to feast on the old man's welcome, and to borrow a thousand pounds he means never to repay.

story.title: Posting to the coronation
story.narration: Then word comes that the old King is dead and Hal is crowned. Certain his hour has struck - 'the laws of England are at my commandment' - Falstaff drops everything and posts for London and the new king's favour.

story.title: I know thee not, old man
story.narration: But the man who kneels to the passing coronation is no longer Hal. Henry V looks down at his old companion and turns him away for good: 'I know thee not, old man. Fall to thy prayers.' The wildness, and the friend, are cast off together - and the reign begins.
